suite.title: The Question Method of Legal Research
suite.subtitle: Interactive Modules
suite.lede: Hands-on companions to the textbook. Some drop you into a live research problem; others build a single skill the work depends on — reading a citation, navigating an annotated code, telling binding authority from persuasive. Together they map onto the book, chapter by chapter.
suite.tagline: Constantly in beta · built by Cas Laskowski

method.intro: The method runs through the whole book — a repeating cycle of five steps and a checkpoint. Some modules drill the entire cycle; others build a single skill the cycle depends on.

steps.d: State the issue as a question that reflects what you currently understand. Be specific — a vague question gives vague results.
Choose the source most likely to answer the type of question you are asking. Search, and read.
In your research log, record the source, what it told you, and how it relates to your question.
Based on what you found, decide what you still need to know — and write it down.
Cycle through the steps until no substantive question remains.

checkpoint.d: Before you close out, review your work: Are your sources current, authoritative, and from the right jurisdiction? Did your sequence of questions capture the full scope of the issue? It is a habit you build into every cycle and perform formally at the end.

parts.name: Foundational Legal Research
parts.blurb: Where the law lives, how to find it, and how to work through each major source type. Chapters 1–9 build the complete foundation for competent legal research.

parts.name: Advanced Legal Research
parts.blurb: The professional obligations and deeper specialties that build on the foundation — ethics, court documents, legislative history, agency materials, practice contexts, AI, and the bar exam.

parts.name: Specialized Legal Research
parts.blurb: Research in particular settings — legal scholarship and foreign, comparative, and international law.

chapters.title: Foundations of Legal Authority
chapters.desc: Where law lives — constitutions, statutes, regulations, cases, and the rest — and how binding and persuasive authority fit together in a hierarchy.

modules.title: How Law Is Made — A 1L Primer
modules.desc: A guided tour of the U.S. sources of law and how they relate: cases, statutes, and regulations — who makes each, how it becomes law, and when to reach for it.

modules.title: Hierarchy of Authorities
modules.desc: A map of the legal authority hierarchy: what makes one source control another, the line between primary and secondary, and why the same case can bind in one courtroom and only persuade in another.

modules.title: Applying the Hierarchy
modules.desc: Drills and applied exercises to test your command of legal authority: sort primary from secondary, tell binding from persuasive, decide when a statute controls, rank a full stack of authorities by binding effect, and place a court in its system.

chapters.title: Introduction to Legal Research Platforms
chapters.desc: The mechanics of searching: natural-language vs. terms-and-connectors, the core Boolean operators, and setting jurisdiction and source filters before you run a query.

modules.title: Database Interface Explorer
modules.desc: Click through a generic legal database — header tools, the search bar, jurisdiction and content filters, the browse tabs, then a results page — and learn what every part does, so you can find the same features on any platform.

modules.title: Using Databases for Legal Research
modules.desc: Read a citation as a retrieval address, pull a known source by location alone, and build a live terms-and-connectors search over a sample library — watching results change against natural language.

modules.title: Database Practice
modules.desc: Short scenarios for the mechanics: where to start in a database, how to change a search to narrow or broaden it, and what a citation does (and does not) need to retrieve a document.

chapters.title: The Question Method of Legal Research
chapters.desc: The engine of the whole book: ask a question, find an answer, write it down, and let that answer reveal the next question — until no substantive question remains.

modules.title: Reverse-Engineering the Question
modules.desc: Take a finished legal paragraph apart into the four research questions that built it, then watch those same questions become a synthesis table and an annotated outline — one color tying each question to its place in every form.

modules.title: Notice of Claim — Research Sandbox
modules.desc: Work a real Arizona notice-of-claim problem through the question → answer → better-question cycle, logging every move in a research log.

modules.title: Working the Chain — Forward & Reverse
modules.desc: Build a chain of research questions forward from a fact pattern, then reverse-engineer the questions behind a finished analysis.

chapters.title: Secondary Sources Research
chapters.desc: Choosing the right secondary source for the question you are holding — encyclopedia, treatise, ALR, Restatement — and mining its footnotes to reach primary authority fast.

modules.title: The Types of Secondary Sources
modules.desc: Explore the nine major secondary sources, then re-sort them by the question they answer, their coverage, and their persuasive weight — and place each on a depth-and-breadth chart.

modules.title: How to Use a Secondary Source
modules.desc: Read a source by the question in front of you: find the sentence that answers it and follow its footnote to primary authority — then recognize the moment to leave the source behind.

modules.title: Secondary Sources Practice
modules.desc: Run one question through the full cycle — match, mine, follow, leave — then practice the trust-and-timing judgment calls, including when an adopted Restatement may earn a cite.

chapters.title: Case Law Research
chapters.desc: Reading opinions critically, separating holding from dictum, and using headnotes, key numbers, and citators to find and validate the cases that govern.

modules.title: Cases as a Web of Answers
modules.desc: How one opinion answers several questions at once — following citations color-coded by question through a four-case web.

modules.title: How to Read a Case
modules.desc: The anatomy of a real appellate opinion (Glik v. Cunniffe), part by part, with the issue and holding tied to Step 1.

modules.title: Case Law Practice
modules.desc: Build a defensible case set, tell holding from dictum, and decide whether a flagged case is still good law.

chapters.title: Statutes Research
chapters.desc: Finding, reading, and updating statutes — and using annotated codes to jump from the text to the cases that interpret it.

modules.title: How to Read a Statute
modules.desc: Read the ADA's public-accommodations rule by its parts, navigate its table of contents and definitions, and let an ambiguity send you to the Notes of Decision.

modules.title: How to Find a Statute
modules.desc: Three finding aids — Popular Name Table, subject Index, and conversion table — each matched to what you already know about a law.

modules.title: Statutes Practice
modules.desc: Find an answer in a section's table of contents, read what a statute's status means for your research, and match a finding aid to what you know.

chapters.title: Basic Regulations and Administrative Research
chapters.desc: Tracing a regulation from its enabling statute to the CFR and the Federal Register — and confirming it is still in force.

modules.title: How to Read a Regulation
modules.desc: Trace a regulation to its authorizing statute, navigate the CFR by its table of contents, read the operative language precisely, and see when the Federal Register matters.

modules.title: Regulations Practice
modules.desc: Navigate the CFR, choose between the CFR and the Federal Register, read shall/must/may/should, and check whether a regulation is still good law.

chapters.title: Researching Across Sources
chapters.desc: Assembling an answer that spans statutes, regulations, and cases at both the federal and state levels — the statute-regulation-case-law triangle.

modules.title: Tracing the Law Across Sources
modules.desc: Three real chains — Rowan, Schechter Poultry, and Arizona's Voter Protection Act — on a swimlane timeline, so you can watch authority pass between statutes, regulations, courts, and the Constitution.

modules.title: Following a Research Hypothesis
modules.desc: Form a hypothesis on a veterans-benefits problem and test it across sources — from a secondary source to the statute, 38 C.F.R. § 3.326, and the case that supplies the argument.

modules.title: Across Sources Practice
modules.desc: Two problems — FMLA and Arizona workers' comp — each solved by pulling a statute, a regulation, and a case, with reveal-for-answer authority.

chapters.title: Narrowing and Closing Out Your Research
chapters.desc: The precision toolkit — proximity and frequency operators — and how to recognize analytical and practical closure so you know when to stop.

modules.title: Building a Precision Search
modules.desc: Turn a research question into a precise Boolean search, watching each connector — OR, quotes, proximity, ATLEAST, a jurisdiction filter — reshape an illustrative result funnel.

modules.title: Knowing When to Stop
modules.desc: Run the closing-out protocol, tell 'no results' from 'no law,' diagnose a stuck search, and draft an exportable research-closure note.

chapters.title: Ethics and Professional Responsibility in Legal Research
chapters.desc: The duty of competent research, candor to the tribunal, and the supervision and verification obligations that ride along with every search.

modules.title: The Competent-Research Duty
modules.desc: Walk scenarios where research — or its absence — triggers Model Rules 1.1, 3.3, 5.1, and 5.3, including the obligation to find adverse authority.
will: Competence as a research duty (Rule 1.1)
Candor and the duty to disclose adverse authority (Rule 3.3)
Supervising others’ research (Rules 5.1 and 5.3)
Build verification into the workflow

chapters.title: Dockets and Court Document Research
chapters.desc: Finding briefs, motions, and docket entries — and what court filings can tell you that opinions cannot.

modules.title: Read a Docket
modules.desc: Trace a case through its docket and pull the filings that reveal its posture, its arguments, and the road to the opinion.
will: What lives in a docket
Find briefs, motions, and orders
Mine briefs for arguments and authorities
Track pending cases and court rules

chapters.title: Legislative History Research
chapters.desc: Reconstructing what a statute meant by tracing bills, committee reports, hearings, and floor debate — and weighing each source correctly.

modules.title: Trace the Bill
modules.desc: Start from a public-law citation and follow a statute back through its legislative record, in chronological order.
will: The legislative process as a research trail
Weigh committee reports, hearings, and floor statements
Find compiled legislative histories first
Use history responsibly — it is persuasive, not binding

chapters.title: Advanced Administrative Research
chapters.desc: Deeper agency work — guidance documents, policy statements, opinion letters, and adjudications — and recording exactly how much weight each carries after Loper Bright.

modules.title: Weigh the Agency Materials
modules.desc: Sort binding regulation from persuasive guidance and advisory letters, logging an authority level for every finding.
will: Rulemaking vs. guidance vs. adjudication
Locate guidance, opinion letters, and ALJ decisions
Record an authority level for every finding
Judicial deference after Loper Bright

chapters.title: Practice-Specific Research Strategies
chapters.desc: How the research cycle’s parameters shift across litigation, transactional, and regulatory practice — the question types, first sources, and closure criteria each demands.

modules.title: Same Cycle, Different Parameters
modules.desc: Run one matter three ways — litigation, transactional, regulatory — and see how the default question type, first source, and closure test change.
will: Default question type by practice context
Where each context starts its research
Closure criteria that differ by practice
Specialized databases for each setting

chapters.title: AI and Legal Research
chapters.desc: Using generative and AI-assisted tools without abdicating judgment — their strengths, failure modes like hallucinated citations, and a verification workflow.

modules.title: Verify the AI’s Work
modules.desc: Run an AI-assisted query through the four-step framework and catch the fabricated authority before it reaches a brief.
will: Where AI helps and where it hallucinates
The four-step framework for AI-assisted research
Verify every AI output against primary authority
The professional-responsibility limits on AI use

chapters.title: The NextGen Bar Exam and Legal Research
chapters.desc: How research skills are tested on the NextGen bar exam, and how the Question Method prepares you for each of the five named competencies.

modules.title: The Five Named Skills
modules.desc: Drill the five NextGen research competencies — ambiguity spotting, source ranking, dispositive-fact identification, gap identification, and closure — under timed conditions.
will: The five named NextGen research skills
Work from a provided, closed set of sources
Reach closure under time pressure
Map exam tasks back to the textbook chapters

chapters.title: Research for Legal Scholarship
chapters.desc: Preemption checks, building a literature base, and the deeper, broader research that scholarly writing demands.

modules.title: Run a Preemption Check
modules.desc: Search the scholarly landscape to confirm your idea has not been done — and build the literature base around it.
will: Run a preemption check
Build and organize a literature base
Use interdisciplinary and historical sources
SSRN, working papers, and citing secondary sources in scholarship

chapters.title: Basic FCIL Research
chapters.desc: A first map of foreign, comparative, and international legal research — treaties, foreign codes, and the finding tools for each.

modules.title: Foreign, Comparative, or International?
modules.desc: Sort a research question into the right FCIL bucket and reach for the finding tool that fits.
will: Foreign vs. comparative vs. international law
Treaties and their research tools
Foreign primary law and finding aids
When to bring in a specialist
